questions: Written by Claudius Ptolemy in Koine Greek, this 2nd-century treatise canonized an Earth-centered cosmos that scholars trusted for more than 1,200 years — which influential work is it?
Almagest: Astronomical treatise by Claudius Ptolemy
This American author of two dozen books pioneered the environmental movement and dreamed up a theory he called 'social ecology' — which thinker, steeped in Hegel, Marx, and Kropotkin, is it?
Sometimes called 'the first American Evangelical,' this Harvard-educated Puritan minister of Boston's Old North helped lead the 1689 revolt against Governor Edmund Andros — who is he?
Published in 1964, years after its author's death, this Hemingway memoir of his hungry 1920s Paris drops in on Gertrude Stein, James Joyce, and the Fitzgeralds — what's its title?
A Moveable Feast: 1964 memoir by Ernest Hemingway
Assembled in 1623 by Shakespeare's colleagues Heminges and Condell, this collection of 36 plays is the reason many of his works survived at all — what do scholars call it?
Mr. William Shakespeare's Comedies, Histories, & Tragedies
First Folio: 1623 collection of William Shakespeare's plays
Founder of the bilingual newspaper Agos and a champion of Turkish–Armenian reconciliation, this journalist was prosecuted three times for 'insulting Turkishness' before his 2007 murder — who was he?
Fill in the blank: “____ (15 April 1721 - 31 October 1765), was the third and youngest son of George II of Great Britain and Ireland and his wife, Caroline of Ansbach.”
Prince William, Duke of Cumberland — ____ (15 April 1721 - 31 October 1765), was the third and youngest son of George II of Great Britain and Ireland and his wife, Caroline of Ansbach.
Fill in the blank: “____ is intertwined with the ____ of the broader Indian subcontinent in South Asia with influences from the surrounding regions of Central, West and East Asia.”
History of Kashmir — ____ is intertwined with the ____ of the broader Indian subcontinent in South Asia with influences from the surrounding regions of Central, West and East Asia.
Fill in the blank: “____ (born February 21, 1964) is an American engineer, retired astronaut, and naval aviator.”
Scott Kelly (astronaut) — ____ (born February 21, 1964) is an American engineer, retired astronaut, and naval aviator.
This British field marshal led the Egyptian Expeditionary Force that captured Beersheba, Jaffa, and Jerusalem from the Ottomans in 1917 — which commander is it?
Edmund Allenby, 1st Viscount Allenby: British Field Marshal (1861–1936)
A León mechanic turned anarcho-syndicalist hero of the 1936 Spanish Revolution, this CNT–FAI militant is remembered as a martyr of the anarchist movement — who is he?
Fill in the blank: “____ (born June 6, 1932) is an American retired test pilot and NASA astronaut who was the seventh person to walk on the Moon.”
David Scott — ____ (born June 6, 1932) is an American retired test pilot and NASA astronaut who was the seventh person to walk on the Moon.
Ruled by the Adil Shahi dynasty in the western Deccan, this powerful state broke from the Bahmani Kingdom in 1490 before Aurangzeb's Mughals swallowed it in 1686 — what was it?
Sultanate of Bijapur: Indian kingdom in the Deccan (1490–1686)
Fill in the blank: “____ is common and often reflects cultural and religious peculiarities as much as geographical and sociological conditions.”
History of alcoholic beverages — ____ is common and often reflects cultural and religious peculiarities as much as geographical and sociological conditions.
What is this — “Piano composition by Claude Debussy”?
Suite bergamasque: Piano composition by Claude Debussy
Fill in the blank: “____ (Cydonia oblonga) is the sole member of the genus Cydonia in the Malinae subtribe (which contains apples, pears, and other fruits) of the Rosaceae family.”
Quince — ____ (Cydonia oblonga) is the sole member of the genus Cydonia in the Malinae subtribe (which contains apples, pears, and other fruits) of the Rosaceae family.
These gram-positive bacteria divide along a single axis, so they grow in pairs or chains — earning a name from the Greek for 'twisted grain' — which genus is it?
Fill in the blank: “____ is a population of the ____ subspecies Panthera tigris tigris native to Northeast China, the Russian Far East, and possibly North Korea.”
Siberian tiger — ____ is a population of the ____ subspecies Panthera tigris tigris native to Northeast China, the Russian Far East, and possibly North Korea.
Sold as Anafranil, this tricyclic antidepressant is best known for treating obsessive–compulsive disorder, but also tackles cataplexy in narcolepsy — which drug is it?
Who is this — “Head of the Catholic Church from 999 to 1003”?
Pope Sylvester II: Head of the Catholic Church from 999 to 1003
Around 1920 this English astrophysicist was the first to correctly guess that stars shine by fusing hydrogen into helium — and a luminosity limit bears his name — who is he?
The last long-necked sauropods standing at the close of the Cretaceous, this group — named for Greek mythology's giants — included the colossal Patagotitan and Argentinosaurus — what's the group?
Fill in the blank: “____ is a sub-discipline of civil ____ in which ____ engineers are trained to design the 'bones and joints' that create the form and shape of human-made structures.”
Structural engineering — ____ is a sub-discipline of civil ____ in which ____ engineers are trained to design the 'bones and joints' that create the form and shape of human-made structures.
Hailed in France as the 'King of Designers,' this German engineer worked with Gottlieb Daimler on the engines behind the first motorcycle and motorboat — who was he?
Wilhelm Maybach: German engine designer and industrialist (1846–1929)
Credited as the first person to tap Iraqi oil, this Armenian businessman secured a permanent 5% slice of Middle Eastern petroleum through the 'Red Line Agreement' — who was he?
What is this — “Chilean archipelago in the Pacific Ocean”?
Juan Fernández Islands: Chilean archipelago in the Pacific Ocean
Fill in the blank: “____ is a major ____ in the interior Pacific Northwest region of the United States.”
Snake River — ____ is a major ____ in the interior Pacific Northwest region of the United States.

Nestled in a fertile mountain valley of western Honduras, this Maya capital flourished from the 5th to 9th centuries before being abandoned and rediscovered by 19th-century excavators — which site is it?
Copán: Maya archaeological site in western Honduras
Famous for the white, peeling bark that gives it its 'silver' nickname, this European deciduous tree has spread to North America, where some states now call it invasive — which species is it?
Nicknamed 'C-7,' this European beetle was shipped to North America to devour aphids, making it one of the most successful aphid-hunters around — which spotted insect is it?
Nicknamed the 'snow monkey,' this brownish-grey, pink-faced primate lives farther north and in colder climates than any other non-human primate — which animal is it?
Japanese macaque: Species of Old World monkey
Fill in the blank: “____ (Megachasma pelagios) is a species of deepwater ____.”
Megamouth shark — ____ (Megachasma pelagios) is a species of deepwater ____.
Once crowned the 'Hop King of the World' and the first mayor of Puyallup, this pioneer kept retracing the Oregon Trail by ox-wagon to preserve its memory — who was he?

Fill in the blank: “____ is the communion of Eastern Christian churches that recognize only three ecumenical councils - the First Council of Nicaea, the First Council of Constantinople and the Council of Ephesus.”

History of Oriental Orthodoxy — ____ is the communion of Eastern Christian churches that recognize only three ecumenical councils - the First Council of Nicaea, the First Council of Constantinople and the Council of Ephesus.
Fill in the blank: “____ (5 October 1930 - 1 December 2007) was a Scottish virologist, a pioneer in virus imaging and identification.”
June Almeida — ____ (5 October 1930 - 1 December 2007) was a Scottish virologist, a pioneer in virus imaging and identification.
Fill in the blank: “____ (named after the Salta region of Argentina where they were first found) is a family of armored herbivorous sauropods from the Upper Cretaceous.”
Saltasauridae — ____ (named after the Salta region of Argentina where they were first found) is a family of armored herbivorous sauropods from the Upper Cretaceous.
Fill in the blank: “____ is located at Pino Suarez 30, a few blocks south of the Zocalo, on what was the Iztapalapa Causeway, near where Hernn Corts and Moctezuma II met for the first time.”
Museum of Mexico City — ____ is located at Pino Suarez 30, a few blocks south of the Zocalo, on what was the Iztapalapa Causeway, near where Hernn Corts and Moctezuma II met for the first time.
This Maltese investigative journalist exposed Panama Papers links in her own country before being killed by a car bomb in 2017 — who was she?
Daphne Caruana Galizia: Maltese journalist and blogger (1964–2017)
This Arab polymath described the pulmonary circulation of blood centuries before William Harvey's 1628 work — overturning Galen on the way — who was he?
ʿAlāʾ al-Dīn Abū al-Ḥasan ʿAlī ibn Abī Ḥazm al-Qarashī
Ibn al-Nafis: Arab polymath and physician (1213–1288)
George Orwell's 1938 memoir recounts his time fighting in the Spanish Civil War — from revolutionary Barcelona to a bullet through the throat in Aragon — what's the book called?
Homage to Catalonia: 1938 memoir by George Orwell
This 9th–10th-century Hanafi jurist from near Samarkand lends his name to one of Sunni Islam's three main schools of theology, alongside Ash'arism and Atharism — who is he?
Abu Mansur al-Maturidi: Islamic scholar and theologian (853–944)
Written by Seneca in retirement after years serving Nero, these 124 surviving letters read like a Stoic diary — and are sometimes simply titled 'Letters from a Stoic' — what's their Latin name?
Epistulae Morales ad Lucilium: Collection of letters by Seneca
In 1863 this German jurist founded the first independent German workers' party, the ADAV, seeding the country's social democratic movement with his brand of state socialism — who was he?
Ferdinand Lassalle: German jurist and socialist (1825–1864)
This baseball manager racked up an untouchable 3,731 wins — nearly 850 more than anyone else — while running the Philadelphia Athletics for their first 50 seasons — who was he?
Connie Mack: American baseball manager and owner (1862–1956)
A Baptist minister turned premier of Saskatchewan, this Scottish-born Canadian gave the continent its first single-payer universal health care program — who was he?
Tommy Douglas: Premier of Saskatchewan from 1944 to 1961
Fill in the blank: “____ was a culturally Persianate, Sunni Muslim state of Turkic mamluk origin.”
Ghaznavid Empire — ____ was a culturally Persianate, Sunni Muslim state of Turkic mamluk origin.
Fill in the blank: “____ were an ancient royal family from modern Karnataka, India, that ruled northern Karnataka and the Konkan from Banavasi in present-day Uttara Kannada district in India.”
Kadamba dynasty — ____ were an ancient royal family from modern Karnataka, India, that ruled northern Karnataka and the Konkan from Banavasi in present-day Uttara Kannada district in India.
This Massachusetts senator was a fierce abolitionist and Radical Republican whose final push for a civil rights bill became a model for the 1964 Civil Rights Act — who was he?
Charles Sumner: American abolitionist and statesman (1811–1874)
Fill in the blank: “____ is reflected through a diverse array of styles of ____.”
music of the United States of America
Music of the United States — ____ is reflected through a diverse array of styles of ____.
Likely composed in the late 1860s by Choctaw Freedmen Wallace and Minerva Willis, this African-American spiritual uses death as a promise of heaven — which beloved hymn is it?
Swing Low, Sweet Chariot: African-American spiritual

This 'bird-hipped' dinosaur clade — named for a pelvis that only superficially resembles a bird's — includes Triceratops, the thick-headed pachycephalosaurs, and the armored stegosaurs — what's the group?
Ornithischia: Extinct clade of primarily herbivorous dinosaurs
Unlike genetics, which studies single genes, this field aims to characterize an organism's entire set of DNA all at once — its structure, function, mapping, and editing — what is it?

Fill in the blank: “____ (d-), meaning "two", (prtos), meaning "first", and (odos), meaning "tooth", is an extinct genus of diprotodontid marsupial from the Pleistocene of Australia containing one species, D.”

Diprotodon — ____ (d-), meaning "two", (prtos), meaning "first", and (odos), meaning "tooth", is an extinct genus of diprotodontid marsupial from the Pleistocene of Australia containing one species, D.
Fill in the blank: “____ (Canis simensis), also called the red jackal, the Simien jackal or Simien fox, is a canine native to the ____ Highlands, including the Simien Mountains.”
Ethiopian wolf — ____ (Canis simensis), also called the red jackal, the Simien jackal or Simien fox, is a canine native to the ____ Highlands, including the Simien Mountains.
This acid-fast rod causes Hansen's disease and is so dependent on its host through reductive evolution that, unlike its tuberculosis cousin, it won't grow in lab media — which bacterium is it?
Mycobacterium leprae: Bacterium that causes leprosy

Breathe in enough of a certain fibrous mineral over years on the job and your lungs slowly inflame and scar, leaving you short of breath and prone to lung cancer — what's the name of this long-term lung disease?
Asbestosis: Pneumoconiosis caused by inhalation and retention of asbestos fibers
Fill in the blank: “____ (binomen: Myrmecobius fasciatus), also known as the noombat or walpurti, is an insectivorous marsupial.”
Numbat — ____ (binomen: Myrmecobius fasciatus), also known as the noombat or walpurti, is an insectivorous marsupial.
Named in 1699 for a minister of Louis XIV, this oval Louisiana body of water is technically an estuary, so its watery title is a slight misnomer — what is it?
Lake Pontchartrain: Estuary located in southeastern Louisiana, United States
What is this — “Set of megalithic sites in Brittany, France”?
Carnac stones: Set of megalithic sites in Brittany, France
Fill in the blank: “____ is an Egyptian ____ located on the west bank of the Nile in ____, Upper Egypt.”
Temple of Edfu — ____ is an Egyptian ____ located on the west bank of the Nile in ____, Upper Egypt.
This $1.3-billion Los Angeles museum campus, opened in 1997, sits atop a hill you reach by a cable-pulled, three-car hovertrain — what is it?
Getty Center: Art museum in Los Angeles, California
What is this — “Rapid transit system in Ontario, Canada”?
Toronto subway: Rapid transit system in Ontario, Canada
Native to the Hindu Kush mountains and prized in India for making hashish, this plant pumps out far higher cannabinoid levels than its industrial-hemp cousins — what is it?

Lurking in the underground limestone waters of the Dinaric Alps, this is Europe's only exclusively cave-dwelling vertebrate — an aquatic salamander that eats, sleeps, and breeds without ever leaving the water. What is it?
A perennial legume native to the Eastern Hemisphere, this tropical pulse is a staple across the Indian Subcontinent and the Caribbean — what is it?
Pigeon pea: Species of perennial legume
Fill in the blank: “____ is an extinct genus of highly predatory macroraptorial sperm whale, containing one known species: L.”
Livyatan — ____ is an extinct genus of highly predatory macroraptorial sperm whale, containing one known species: L.

Fill in the blank: “____ (Equus grevyi), also known commonly as the imperial ____, is the largest living species of wild equid and the most threatened of the three species of zebras, the other two being the plains ____ and the mountain ____.”

Grévy's zebra — ____ (Equus grevyi), also known commonly as the imperial ____, is the largest living species of wild equid and the most threatened of the three species of zebras, the other two being the plains ____ and the mountain ____.
Fill in the blank: “____ are the largest family of butterflies, with more than 6,000 species distributed throughout most of the world.”
Nymphalidae — ____ are the largest family of butterflies, with more than 6,000 species distributed throughout most of the world.
Fill in the blank: “____ (Mapudungun pd or pdu) are two species of South American deer from the genus ____, and are the world's smallest deer.”
Pudu — ____ (Mapudungun pd or pdu) are two species of South American deer from the genus ____, and are the world's smallest deer.
What is this — “Oldest surviving literary text in the world”?
Liturgy to Nintud on the creation of man and woman
Kesh temple hymn: Oldest surviving literary text in the world
Fill in the blank: “____ (18 February 1559 - 1 July 1614) was a classical scholar and philologist, first in France and then later in England.”
Isaac Casaubon — ____ (18 February 1559 - 1 July 1614) was a classical scholar and philologist, first in France and then later in England.
Fill in the blank: “____ (____; born October 11, 1946) is a Japanese former gymnast and one of the most successful Olympic athletes of all time.”
Sawao Katō — ____ (____; born October 11, 1946) is a Japanese former gymnast and one of the most successful Olympic athletes of all time.
Fill in the blank: “____ was a non-violent ____ for Samoan independence from colonial rule during the first half of the 20th century.”
Mau movement — ____ was a non-violent ____ for Samoan independence from colonial rule during the first half of the 20th century.
A close ally of Robespierre, this French revolutionary chose suicide over arrest when authorities stormed the Hôtel de Ville during the coup of 9–10 Thermidor — who was he?
Inspired by a belt of daffodils Wordsworth and his sister Dorothy spotted by Ullswater, this 24-line lyric placed fifth in a 1995 BBC poll of Britain's favorite poems — what's its title?
I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud: Lyric poem by William Wordsworth
I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
Who is this — “Missionary, archbishop, and saint (died 604)”?
Augustine of Canterbury: Missionary, archbishop, and saint (died 604)

Fill in the blank: “____ (simplified; traditional; pinyin: shumhu) is a type of Chinese ____ brush ____ which uses washes of black ____, such as that used in East Asian calligraphy, in different concentrations.”

Ink wash painting — ____ (simplified; traditional; pinyin: shumhu) is a type of Chinese ____ brush ____ which uses washes of black ____, such as that used in East Asian calligraphy, in different concentrations.
George W. Bush's deputy chief of staff and an architect of the Iraq War, this consultant earned the nickname "the Architect" in Bush's 2004 victory speech — who is he?
Karl Rove: American political consultant and policy advisor (born 1950)
For 37 years this Village Voice music critic ran the annual Pazz & Jop poll and was an early champion of hip hop and riot grrrl — who is he?

Standing 6 feet 7 inches tall, this Antiguan fast bowler used his towering height to make the ball bounce alarmingly high, taking 405 Test wickets while famously refusing to talk to journalists — who is he?
A satrap of Bactria who murdered Darius III and crowned himself Artaxerxes V, this man tried to hold the eastern Achaemenid Empire against Alexander the Great — who was he?
Bessus: Achaemenid satrap and pretender to throne (died 329 BC)
Mongol invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire
Mongol invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire
Mongol invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire: 1219–1221 military campaign
Mongol invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire
Established around 939 after Viking armies were driven from the northwestern peninsula of France, this medieval feudal state survived until 1547 — what was it?
Duchy of Brittany: Medieval feudal state in northwest France
Sparked in Khorasan in the mid-8th century, this uprising led by descendants of Muhammad's uncle toppled the Umayyad Caliphate and founded a new one — what is it called?
Abbasid revolution: 747–750 overthrow of the Umayyad caliphate
Fill in the blank: “____ is now ____ begins with evidence of a Neanderthal presence from at least 230,000 years ago, while Homo sapiens arrived by about 31,000 BC.”
History of Wales — ____ is now ____ begins with evidence of a Neanderthal presence from at least 230,000 years ago, while Homo sapiens arrived by about 31,000 BC.
One of the three leaders of the July 1936 coup that began the Spanish Civil War, this general coined the phrase "fifth column" before dying in a plane crash — who was he?
Emilio Mola y Vidal, 1st Duke of Mola, Grandee of Spain
What is this — “Early study of triangles can be traced to Egyptian mathematics (Rhind Mathematical Papyrus) and Babylonian mathematics during the 2nd millennium BC.”?
History of trigonometry: Early study of triangles can be traced to Egyptian mathematics (Rhind Mathematical Papyrus) and Babylonian mathematics during the 2nd millennium BC.
Famous for ruthless contract negotiations and founder of ABKCO, this businessman managed the Beatles and the Rolling Stones simultaneously — who was he?
Allen Klein: American businessman and talent manager (1931–2009)
Formed in the South Bronx in 1978 and known for turntablism and conscious lyrics, this hip-hop group broke through in 1982 with "The Message" — who are they?
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five: American hip hop group (1978–1988)
Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five
Fill in the blank: “____ is a genre of electronic dance ____ (EDM) that emerged in London in the early 2000s.”
Grime music — ____ is a genre of electronic dance ____ (EDM) that emerged in London in the early 2000s.
Fill in the blank: “____ (SGCC), commonly known as the ____, is a Chinese ____-owned electric utility ____.”
State Grid Corporation of China — ____ (SGCC), commonly known as the ____, is a Chinese ____-owned electric utility ____.
A white crystalline reducing agent (formula NaBH4) used in papermaking and dye industries, this compound was discovered in the 1940s during a wartime hunt for volatile uranium compounds — what is it?

Absorbing the former goosefoot family and spanning some 2,040 species, this is the most species-rich lineage in the order Caryophyllales — what flowering-plant family is it, named for its type genus of showy tassels?
One of the 48 constellations Ptolemy listed, this small star pattern was often drawn as a vulture or eagle carrying a stringed instrument, earning it the nickname "Falling Vulture" — what is it?
Lyra: Constellation in the northern celestial hemisphere
Moved by the Holocaust and the Eichmann trial, this Yale social psychologist ran a famously controversial 1961 experiment on obedience in a basement lab — who was he?
The 1924 discovery of the "Taung child" by anatomist Raymond Dart gave the world its first early hominin fossil — what South African species did it belong to?
Australopithecus africanus: Extinct hominid from South Africa
Fill in the blank: “____ is an abnormal immune response to ____.”
Food allergy — ____ is an abnormal immune response to ____.
Just under 12 light-years away in the constellation Cetus, this Sun-like, metal-poor star is the closest solitary G-type star to us and visible to the naked eye — what is it?
Tau Ceti: Single yellow-hued star in the constellation Cetus
Harry Seeley split the dinosaurs into two groups in 1888 by their hip structure; this "reptile-hipped" division takes its name from Greek words for lizard and hip joint — what is it?
This North Wales national park contains all 15 Welsh mountains over 3,000 feet, including the country's tallest peak — what is this mountainous region called?
Snowdonia: Mountainous region and national park in North Wales
Fill in the blank: “____ (Portuguese) is a volcanic ____ in the Azores archipelago of Portugal, 1,181 km (734 mi) due west of Lisbon.”
Terceira Island — ____ (Portuguese) is a volcanic ____ in the Azores archipelago of Portugal, 1,181 km (734 mi) due west of Lisbon.
The only living member of the genus Pseudorca, this highly social oceanic dolphin gets its misleading name because its skull resembles that of the orca — what is it?
False killer whale: Species of oceanic dolphin in the genus Pseudorca
The only marsupial found north of Mexico — ranging up into southern Canada — this nocturnal, cat-sized opportunist is more likely than most animals in its range to end up as roadkill. What is it?
Virginia opossum: Species of North American marsupial
Carried harmlessly in the nasopharynx of about 10% of adults, this round, pair-forming Gram-negative bacterium causes the only form of bacterial meningitis known to occur in epidemics — what is it?
Neisseria meningitidis: Species of bacterium that can cause meningitis

Native to Central Europe but introduced to the British Isles by 1500 — where it's simply called the sycamore — this wind-tolerant maple now spreads so freely it's deemed invasive in places. What's its scientific name?
Acer pseudoplatanus: Species of maple tree
Fill in the blank: “____ (May 12, 1905 - January 10, 1989) was an American journalist whose charged radio report on the Hindenburg disaster is recognized as a landmark in broadcasting.”
Herbert Morrison (journalist) — ____ (May 12, 1905 - January 10, 1989) was an American journalist whose charged radio report on the Hindenburg disaster is recognized as a landmark in broadcasting.
Fill in the blank: “____ is a family of ornithischian dinosaurs that were likely among the most basal (primitive) members of the group.”
Heterodontosauridae — ____ is a family of ornithischian dinosaurs that were likely among the most basal (primitive) members of the group.

Fill in the blank: “____ (September 24, 1946), better known as "Mean" ____, is an American former professional football defensive tackle who played for the Pittsburgh Steelers of the National Football League (NFL) from 1969 to 1981.”

Joe Greene — ____ (September 24, 1946), better known as "Mean" ____, is an American former professional football defensive tackle who played for the Pittsburgh Steelers of the National Football League (NFL) from 1969 to 1981.
Written by Claudio Monteverdi in 1607 for a Carnival at Mantua and based on the legend of Orpheus, this is the earliest opera still regularly performed — what is it?
Emma Lazarus wrote this 1883 sonnet to raise money for a pedestal, and in 1903 its words were cast onto a bronze plaque inside the Statue of Liberty — what is the poem's title?
The New Colossus: Sonnet by Emma Lazarus, inscribed at the Statue of Liberty
Spanning some 7,400 lines of dactylic hexameter, this first-century BCE poem by Lucretius set out to explain Epicurean philosophy and atomism to a Roman audience — what is it called?
De rerum natura: 1st-century BC didactic poem by Lucretius

Credited with founding feminist psychology, this German-born psychoanalyst pushed back on Freud's notion of penis envy, tracing the differences between men and women to society rather than biology — who was she?

Flourishing in Bruges and Ghent from the 1420s of Robert Campin and Jan van Eyck, the artists of this Northern Renaissance movement were once dubbed the "Flemish Primitives" — what is this body of work called?
Early Netherlandish painting: 15th and 16th-century art of the Low Countries
Fill in the blank: “____ (zuh-; Ancient; c.”
He retired in 1997 with 879 victories — then the NCAA Division I men's basketball record — after 36 years and two national titles at North Carolina. Which Tar Heel coaching legend?
This Welsh theologian and poet became the 104th Archbishop of Canterbury, and the first in modern times not appointed from within the Church of England. Who is he?
Rowan Williams, Baron Williams of Oystermouth
Rowan Williams: Archbishop of Canterbury from 2003 to 2012
What is this — “Gaelic kingdom in western Scotland and northeastern Ireland”?
Dál Riata: Gaelic kingdom in western Scotland and northeastern Ireland
Fill in the blank: “____ is a type of economic system involving social ownership of the means of production within the framework of a ____ economy.”
Market socialism — ____ is a type of economic system involving social ownership of the means of production within the framework of a ____ economy.
Fill in the blank: “____ is now ____ more than 10,000 years ago, as evidenced by the discovery of the remains of prehistoric Leanderthal Lady.”
History of Texas — ____ is now ____ more than 10,000 years ago, as evidenced by the discovery of the remains of prehistoric Leanderthal Lady.
Born of African traditions and the experience of slavery, this genre of Christian folk song blended work songs and plantation songs that later evolved into the blues and gospel. What's the genre?
For Donna Summer's 1977 album where each track evoked a different decade, Moroder and Bellotte reached for a Moog synthesizer to conjure a 'futuristic' mood — birthing which chart-topping single?
I Feel Love: 1977 single by Donna Summer
Fill in the blank: “____ comprises a variety of genres of ____ regularly performed or heard around the ____ season.”
Christmas music — ____ comprises a variety of genres of ____ regularly performed or heard around the ____ season.
What is this — “1963 single by the Beatles”?
I Want to Hold Your Hand
I Want to Hold Your Hand
I Want to Hold Your Hand: 1963 single by the Beatles
I Want to Hold Your Hand
Fill in the blank: “____ (NLCs), or night shining clouds, are tenuous ____-like phenomena in the upper atmosphere.”
Noctilucent cloud — ____ (NLCs), or night shining clouds, are tenuous ____-like phenomena in the upper atmosphere.
What is this — “High blood sugar levels during pregnancy”?
Gestational diabetes: High blood sugar levels during pregnancy

Known mostly from a single lectotype skull catalogued KNM-ER 1470, this archaic East African human from about 2 million years ago is so debated that some would reclassify it under Australopithecus or Kenyanthropus. Which species?
Homo rudolfensis: Extinct hominin from the Early Pleistocene of East Africa
This NYU social psychologist built moral foundations theory, arguing our moral reasoning springs from innate gut feelings rather than cool logic. Who is he?
Records of the Three Kingdoms credit this late-Han physician as the first in China to use a general anaesthetic in surgery — a wine-and-herb brew called mafeisan. Who was he?
One of Ptolemy's original 48, this northern winter constellation takes its Latin name from a 'charioteer' and contributes a star to the Winter Hexagon. Which constellation?
Auriga: Constellation in the northern celestial hemisphere

In 1933 she inspired a Katharine Hepburn film character, and earlier became the first woman to fly solo from London to Australia — before vanishing over the Thames Estuary in 1941. Who was this pioneering pilot?
Discovered in 1985, this black solid of 60 carbon atoms forms a truncated-icosahedron cage of twenty hexagons and twelve pentagons — and is nicknamed a 'buckyball.' What is it?
This Indian dairy engineer set up a milk cooperative at Anand in 1950 that became Amul, sparking the country's 'White Revolution' in milk production. Who was he?
Published by Georg Cantor in 1891, this elegant proof shows some infinite sets can't be matched one-to-one with the natural numbers — proving uncountable sets exist. What's the proof called?
Cantor's diagonal argument: Proof in set theory
The New Yorker called her 'learned but accessible,' and her media presence has earned her the title of 'Britain's best-known classicist.' Which Cambridge scholar of Ancient Rome?
Fill in the blank: “____ (sham-PLAYN) is a large natural freshwater ____ in North America.”
Lake Champlain — ____ (sham-PLAYN) is a large natural freshwater ____ in North America.
What is this — “Neolithic archaeological site in Scotland”?

This Manchester-born English novelist trained as a lawyer but won fame in 1834 with Rookwood, which made the highwayman Dick Turpin its leading character — who was he?

This English journalist is forever tied to her 1861 Book of Household Management, though she'd cheerfully plagiarised the recipes for her husband's magazine from other works — who was she, better known by her married title?
Isabella Beeton: English journalist, publisher and writer (1836–1865)

Born into extreme poverty in Michigan and taught to read and write by herself, this convent-educated woman turned anti-authoritarian and became an American anarchist feminist writer and speaker — who was she?
Voltairine de Cleyre: American anarchist writer and feminist (1866–1912)
Born into Malay aristocracy in North Sumatra and recalled home in 1937 to marry a sultan's daughter, this poet became a National Hero of Indonesia — who was he?

Dublin-born and New York-raised, this Beaux-Arts sculptor won acclaim for Civil War monuments like the Robert Gould Shaw Memorial on Boston Common and his Abraham Lincoln: The Man — who was he?
Augustus Saint-Gaudens: American sculptor and engraver (1848–1907)
A wildly popular prizefighter in 1790s Britain, he helped pioneer scientific boxing technique, even publishing two books on the art — who was this English boxer?

Musa of Parthia — ____ (also spelled Mousa), also known as Thea ____, was the reigning queen of the Parthian Empire from 2 BC to 4 AD.
He landed at Anzac Cove in 1915, distinguished himself planning the Battle of Pozières, and remains the only Australian ever to reach the rank of field marshal — who was he?

As Ulysses S. Grant's most trusted advisor and secretary of state, this New York statesman settled the Alabama Claims with Britain and helped pioneer the idea of international arbitration — who was he?
This Hyksos king ruled Lower Egypt during the Fifteenth Dynasty for some forty years, outliving his southern rival Kamose but not Ahmose I — the Greeks called him Apophis. What was his Egyptian name?

Who is this — “Queen of England from 1135 to 1152”?
Matilda of Boulogne: Queen of England from 1135 to 1152

A former Tasmanian schoolteacher who'd led the state's Labor branch and been its premier, he later founded the United Australia Party and served as the tenth prime minister of Australia until his death in 1939 — who was he?
Joseph Lyons: Prime Minister of Australia from 1932 to 1939

Rising from vice president after Bingu wa Mutharika's death, she became the first female president of Malawi in 2012 and only the second woman to lead an African nation, after Liberia's Ellen Johnson Sirleaf — who is she?

An ethnic Tutsi army officer who'd helped crush a 1965 Hutu coup, he served as the very last Prime Minister of the Kingdom of Burundi before becoming his country's first president — and ruling as military strongman for a decade until his 1976 overthrow. Who was he?

Arriving in 1946 to lead a respected-but-undersized orchestra still reeling from the war, this Austro-Hungarian-born American conductor turned the Cleveland Orchestra into what critics called one of the world's finest. Who was the maestro?

The lone member of its genus, this Asian wild canid breaks the dog mold with a convex skull, a missing third lower molar, and single-cusped upper molars — and once roamed all the way into Europe. What animal is it?

Sitting at the heart of paleontology's so-called "muddle in the middle," this archaic human — named by Otto Schoetensack in 1908 from a single jawbone found in a German sand pit — may be the last common ancestor of us, Neanderthals, and Denisovans. Which species is it?
Homo heidelbergensis: Extinct species of archaic human

One humble plant species, when bred into different cultivars, gives us table sugar, the crimson root in your salad, the leafy chard on your plate, and cattle fodder all at once. What flowering plant pulls off this quadruple act?
Beta vulgaris: Species of flowering plant

A heavy-bodied, non-venomous South American snake and a staple of private collections, it's prized for a color pattern that's wildly variable yet instantly recognizable. Which member of the family Boidae is it?

This Canadian-American inventor pulled off the first transmission of speech by radio in 1900 and the first two-way radiotelegraphy across the Atlantic in 1906, racking up hundreds of patents — seven granted after his death. Who was this AM-radio pioneer?

A French economist who gave up accounting to lecture across France, he founded peace societies and the Inter-Parliamentary Union before sharing the very first Nobel Peace Prize in 1901. Who was he?

This Zambian-born economist turned heads with four New York Times bestsellers, including "Dead Aid," which argues that foreign aid actually holds Africa back. Which author and life peer is it?

This Irish experimental physicist shared the 1951 Nobel with John Cockcroft for splitting the atom — the first nuclear transmutation, as the prize committee put it, "entirely under human control." Who was he?
A founder of modern topology who also wrote under the playful pen name "Paul Mongré," this German mathematician — Jewish and facing deportation — died by suicide with his family in 1942. Who was he?

Geology of Mars — ____ is the scientific study of the surface, crust, and interior of the planet ____.

Writing under a pen name, this Belgian-Australian sinologist scandalized 1970s intellectuals with a trilogy denouncing China's Cultural Revolution and the West's idolizing of Mao. Whose pseudonym was it?
Simon Leys: Belgian-Australian writer, sinologist and essayist (1935–2014)
Credited as the first to create a transuranium element — neptunium — this Berkeley physicist shared the 1951 Nobel in Chemistry with Glenn Seaborg. Who was he?

Arthur Phillip — ____ (11 October 1738 - 31 August 1814) was a British Royal Navy officer who served as the first governor of the Colony of New South Wales.

The first chapter of Wagner's epic "Ring" cycle, this music drama premiered alone in Munich in 1869 — though Wagner had actually written its libretto last, and dead last in the cycle. Which opera is it?

Sexual dysfunction — ____ is difficulty experienced by an individual or partners during any stage of normal ____ activity, including physical pleasure, desire, preference, arousal, or orgasm.
Fill in the blank: “____ (Chroicocephalus ridibundus) is a small ____ that breeds in much of the Palearctic in Europe and Asia, and also locally in smaller numbers in coastal eastern Canada.”
Black-headed gull — ____ (Chroicocephalus ridibundus) is a small ____ that breeds in much of the Palearctic in Europe and Asia, and also locally in smaller numbers in coastal eastern Canada.

A self-described "sugarholic" and "junk food junkie" until age 15, he turned his life around after a nutrition lecture and went on to host the first national fitness TV show, earning the title "Godfather of Fitness." Who was he?
Jack LaLanne: American fitness and nutrition guru and motivational speaker (1914–2011)

Van Gogh Museum — ____ (Dutch) is a Dutch art ____ dedicated to the works of Vincent ____ and his contemporaries in the ____ Square in Amsterdam South, close to the Stedelijk ____, the Rijksmuseum, and the Concertgebouw.

Born in Boston, this colonial portrait painter of wealthy New Englanders moved to London in 1774 — never returning to America — and pioneered history paintings in modern dress. Who was he?

This German experimental physicist shared the 1954 Nobel Prize in Physics with Max Born for the 'coincidence method' — the circuitry he used to study the Compton effect and cosmic rays. Who was he?

A Founding Father who signed the Treaty of Paris and directed U.S. foreign policy through much of the 1780s, he became the very first chief justice of the United States in 1789 — who was he?

Crowned the 'King of Swing,' this clarinetist led one of the first integrated jazz groups and played a 1938 Carnegie Hall concert hailed as jazz's 'coming out' party — who was he?

Honey bee — ____ (also spelled honeybee) is a eusocial flying insect from the genus Apis of the largest ____ family, Apidae.
A cold-sensitive perennial herb with sweet pine and citrus flavours, it's so close to oregano that in some Middle Eastern countries the two share a name — what is it?
Named for a Danish mathematician and proved in 1906, this result states that the convex transformation of a mean is at most the mean of the convex transformation — what is it?

A colourless, flammable liquid with a detergent-like odor, this non-polar cycloalkane is mainly used to make adipic acid and caprolactam — precursors to nylon. What is it?

Marlon James (novelist) — ____ (born 24 November 1970) is a Jamaican writer.

What is this — “2017 single by Luis Fonsi featuring Daddy Yankee”?
Despacito: 2017 single by Luis Fonsi featuring Daddy Yankee
Usually viral and often worse at night, this childhood respiratory infection swells the trachea and announces itself with an unmistakable barking, brassy cough — what's it called?

Before becoming TV's most famous bow-tied science educator, this Boeing mechanical engineer invented a hydraulic resonance suppressor tube for 747s, then quit to do sketch comedy. Who is he?

More than 80% of cases of this cancer — which grows in the thin lining covering the lungs and other organs — trace back to asbestos exposure. What is it?
Discovered in 1945 and first sold in 1964, this class of beta-lactam antibiotics was originally pulled from a fungus once known as Cephalosporium — what's the family called?
What is this — “Organic compound and a form of vitamin B3”?
Nicotinic acid: Organic compound and a form of vitamin B3

Madagascar's largest carnivore is a slender, long-tailed mammal that climbs down trees head-first and has convergently evolved so many cat-like features it's been likened to a small cougar — yet it's no cat. What is it?
Fossa (animal): Cat-like, carnivorous mammal endemic to Madagascar
Bordered by Lake Michigan, the Mississippi, and the Ohio rivers, this Midwestern state pairs a modest capital in Springfield with a sprawling metropolis in its northeast corner — which state is it?
The only U.S. state that borders just one other state, this rugged New England giant touches two Canadian provinces and is the most rural of the fifty — which state is it?

The least populous state despite ranking tenth in size, this Rocky Mountain land splits between mountain rangelands in the west and High Plains prairie in the east, with Cheyenne as its capital — which state?
Nicknamed the Yellowhammer State after its state bird and also known as the "Heart of Dixie," this Deep South state runs from the mountainous Tennessee Valley down to Mobile Bay — which state is it?
The only U.S. state that divides itself into "parishes" instead of counties, this Deep South state wears its French heritage proudly, from Baton Rouge to New Orleans — which state is it?
Culturally and legally split into three "Grand Divisions" — East, Middle, and West — this landlocked Southern state is anchored by its capital and largest city, Nashville. Which state is it?
Fill in the blank: “____ (born January 20, 1960) is an American video game designer and co-founder of the game development company Maxis, which later became part of Electronic Arts.”
Will Wright (game designer) — ____ (born January 20, 1960) is an American video game designer and co-founder of the game development company Maxis, which later became part of Electronic Arts.
In 1929 she became the very first Best Actress Oscar winner — and the only one ever to take the prize for three different film roles at once. Which silent-era star was she?
This French director spent six decades probing memory and consciousness on film, from the concentration-camp documentary Night and Fog to Hiroshima mon amour and Last Year at Marienbad — who is he?

Norman Wisdom: English actor, comedian and singer (1915–2010)
Dr. Seuss wrote this beloved book to win a $50 bet that he couldn't craft a gripping children's story using only 50 words — and Sam-I-am was the result. Which book is it?

Laurence Sterne's digressive comic "memoir" begins with its narrator's conception and somehow doesn't get around to his birth until the third volume — which novel is this?

The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman
The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman: 1759–1767 novel by Laurence Sterne
The Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy, Gentleman

Hailed as perhaps "the greatest artist-craftsman of the twentieth century" as a sculptor and typeface designer, this Englishman is also a deeply controversial figure following revelations of his abuse — who was he?
Arthur Eric Rowton Peter Joseph Gill
Arthur Eric Rovton Peter Joseph Gill
His debut satire of war and bureaucracy gave the English language a phrase for an absurd no-win choice — and twice earned him a Nobel Prize nomination. Which author was he?

One of the great Mexican muralists alongside Rivera and Orozco, this social-realist painter also took up arms as a lieutenant colonel for the Spanish Republic and later led a failed assassination attempt — who was he?

One of only five players ever to score 50 goals in 50 games, this sniper racked up 741 career goals — fifth-most in NHL history — and won Cups with Dallas and Detroit. Who is he?

Nicknamed "the Rocket," this Montreal Canadiens legend was the first player in NHL history to score 50 goals in a single season — and the first to reach 500 for his career. Who was he?
Maurice Richard: Canadian ice hockey player (1921–2000)
A towering figure whose political career spanned seven decades, this Greek statesman served four terms as prime minister and two as president of the Third Hellenic Republic — who was he?

Siege of Carthage (Third Punic War) — ____ was the main engagement of the Third Punic War fought between ____ and Rome.

John III of Portugal — ____ (6 June 1502 - 11 June 1557), nicknamed The Pious, was the King of ____ and the Algarve from 1521 until he died in 1557.

James Dobson: American evangelical Christian psychologist and author (1936–2025)

Experimental music — ____ is a general label for any ____ or ____ genre that pushes existing boundaries and genre definitions.

The largest of the true foxes and the most widely distributed carnivore on Earth, this Northern Hemisphere native is so destructive where it's been introduced that it made the list of the world's 100 worst invasive species — which animal is it?

Arriving late to a lecture, this mathematician copied down two famous unsolved problems in statistics, mistook them for homework — and solved them; he's better known for inventing the simplex algorithm for linear programming. Who was he?

malignant neoplasm of oral cavity NOS
malignant neoplasm of other specified parts of mouth
malignant neoplasm of lip, oral cavity and pharynx

Andijan — ____ (-JAHN), also spelt Andijon and formerly), is a city in Uzbekistan.
Born from an undersea eruption that breached the surface off Iceland's coast in 1963, this volcanic island has been shrinking from wave erosion ever since reaching its peak size. What is it?

Praised for its realistic portrait of a working-class family in fictional Lanford, Illinois, this ABC sitcom hit No. 1 in the Nielsen ratings in its second season — then got a short-lived revival in 2018. What was it?
Before creating one of TV's most enduring science-fiction universes, this writer flew 89 combat missions in World War II, worked as a commercial pilot, and joined the LAPD. Who was he?

Nicknamed "the Tiger," this militant member of the Committee of Public Safety helped architect the Reign of Terror — then turned on Robespierre and helped bring him down on 9 Thermidor. Who was he?

Thrash metal — ____ (or simply ____) is an extreme subgenre of heavy ____ music characterized by its overall aggression and fast tempo.
Tracing its roots to a service called PlayNET, this web portal grew to about three million users by 1995, displacing rivals like CompuServe and Prodigy as the largest online service. What is it?

Thrombosis: Formation of blood clots inside the blood vessels

Atlantic cod — ____ (pl.: ____; Gadus morhua) is a fish of the family Gadidae, widely consumed by humans.

Bordered by Virginia, Tennessee, Georgia, and the Atlantic, this state pairs its capital Raleigh with Charlotte, one of the fastest-growing cities in the country. Which state is it?

Kaiser Heinrich V. Heiliges Römisches Reich

Noise music — ____ (or simply ____) is a subgenre of experimental ____ that is characterised by its use of unwanted ____ as a primary musical element.

Starting out as a child in the vaudeville act "The Four Cohans," this showman went on to write more than 50 Broadway shows and 300 songs, including "Over There" and "Give My Regards to Broadway." Who was he?

Meitnerium: Chemical element with atomic number 109 (Mt)
Fill in the blank: “____ is the process by which a chemical or physical ____ is transmitted through a cell as a series of molecular events.”

This American physicist worked with Niels Bohr on nuclear fission and had a gift for coinages — "quantum foam," "wormhole," and the term that popularized "gravitationally collapsed objects." Who was he?
John Archibald Wheeler: American theoretical physicist (1911–2008)

Fayetteville, North Carolina — ____ (locally) is a city in and the county seat of Cumberland County, ____, United States.

General Hospital: American television soap opera (since 1963)

Gilligan's Island — ____ is an American sitcom created and produced by Sherwood Schwartz.

Created by Reg Watson and on the air from 1985 to 2025, this Melbourne-set serial was cancelled by one network after just four months — then revived by a rival and grew into Australia's longest-running drama. What is it?
What is this — “1837 fairy tale by Hans Christian Andersen”?

H. G. Wells's 1895 novella sends a Victorian scientist hurtling to the year 802,701 — and along the way coined the very term we now use for any such vehicle. What is the book's title?
The Time Machine: 1895 dystopian science fiction novella by H. G. Wells

Fill in the blank: “____ (November 24, 1885 - March 29, 1970) was an American journalist and activist, best known for her reporting on and support for communist movements in the Soviet Union and the People's Republic of China.”

Taken by mouth, smeared as a cream, or injected, this antiviral medication is a frontline treatment for herpes simplex, chickenpox, and shingles. What is it?

Found across Africa, Asia, and Australia, this family of nonvenomous snakes — including some of the world's largest — must coil around prey and squeeze it into cardiac arrest before swallowing it whole. What is the family?

Fill in the blank: “____ (23 March 1769 - 28 August 1839) was an English geologist, credited with creating the first detailed, nationwide geological map of any country.”
William Smith (geologist) — ____ (23 March 1769 - 28 August 1839) was an English geologist, credited with creating the first detailed, nationwide geological map of any country.

This fourth-largest state splits its personality down the middle — Rocky Mountain ranges to the west, prairie and badlands to the east — with Helena as its capital but Billings as its biggest city. Which one is it?
